front: Work: opens with the public execution of Robert-Francois Damiens; observes how schools and hospitals resemble Bentham's Panopticon
Concept: the push to create bodies that 'may be subjected, used, transformed and improved' through the modern carceral system
Concept: the state's control over the bodies of its populace, defined as the 'death he is capable of requiring'; introduced in The Will to Knowledge
work: The History of Sexuality and Biopower

front: Philosopher: Achille Mbembe expanded this thinker's concept of biopower into 'necropolitics' — the ability to determine who lives or dies
work: The History of Sexuality and Biopower

front: Philosopher: discovered and republished memoirs by the intersex writer Herculine Barbin; described them as living in 'the happy limbo of a non-identity'
work: The History of Sexuality and Biopower

front: Work: opens with a celebrated analysis of Velazquez's Las Meninas; subtitled 'An Archaeology of the Human Sciences'; introduces epistemes
work: The Order of Things and Epistemes

front: Concept: historical groundings of knowledge within a particular epoch; Jean Piaget paired this concept with Kuhn's notion of paradigms
work: The Order of Things and Epistemes

front: Concept: counter-spaces of deviation that first appear in the preface of The Order of Things; Samuel Delany used this term in Trouble on Triton
work: The Order of Things and Epistemes

front: Work: argues the 'Great Confinement' of the insane began in the Age of Reason; opens with the 'Ship of Fools' motif
Philosopher: Derrida responded to this thinker with an essay pairing the cogito with madness; this thinker added the appendix 'My Body, This Paper, This Fire'
Work: develops the 'archaeological method' for analyzing how epistemes and discursive formations are governed by subconscious rules
work: The Archaeology of Knowledge and Methodology

front: Work: defined the 'medical gaze' and traced the shift from 'What's the matter with you?' to 'Where does it hurt?'
Philosopher: debated Noam Chomsky on Dutch TV in 1971; the host tried to make him wear a red wig; he was partly paid in hashish
Philosopher: Habermas called this thinker a 'crypto-normativist' who relied on Enlightenment principles while trying to refute them
